EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A noreaster moved up the coast southeast of Cape Cod and spread snow across much of Massachusetts, northern Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southern New Hampshire.
Strong southwest flow behind a warm front allowed for strong winds at the surface along the east coast of Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator recorded a wind gust of 60 mph on a home weather station.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Great Lakes across central New England spreading snow across much of Southern New England.

Low pressure moved up the Atlantic coast and spread snow across southern New England.  Heavy snow fell across much of southern New Hampshire, downing trees and power lines.  At Manchester-Boston Regional Airport, more than a dozen flights were cancelled.

An intensifying coastal low spread heavy snow across southern New England. Snow began in the early afternoon across Connecticut and southwestern Massachusetts, spreading quickly across Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and southern New Hampshire. Eight to twelve inches of snow fell across much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary dropped south of New England on the evening of the 10th. Low pressure developed along the frontal boundary across the southeastern states late on the night of the 10th into the 11th.  The low then tracked rapidly to the northeast, spreading a significant amount of precipitation into New England. A deep layer of warm air aloft and sub-freezing air at the surface resulted in a major ice storm across interior Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire as well as much of northern New England. ||The hardest hit areas in southern New England were the Monadnock region of southwest New Hampshire, the Worcester Hills in central Massachusetts, and the east slopes of the Berkshires in western Massachusetts. Anywhere from half an inch to an inch of ice accreted on many exposed surfaces. Especially when combined with breezy conditions, the ice downed numerous trees, branches, and power lines which resulted in widespread power outages. More than 300,000 customers were reportedly without power in Massachusetts and an additional 300,000 were without power in the state of New Hampshire. Because of the breadth of this storm (from Pennsylvania to Maine), extra crews to reinstate power were harder to come by.  Power crews from states as far away as South Carolina, as well as local National Guard teams, were called out to help with power restoration and clean up.  While most people had their power restored within a week, others were still without power at Christmas (nearly 2 weeks later). During this period, temperatures were mostly below normal and at least one major snowstorm affected the same area.  At the time of the December 19th snowstorm, over 100,000 customers were still without power in the two states combined.  Most communities had opened shelters to provide a warm, safe place for residents.  According to the numbers reported in local newspapers, most did not take advantage of this opportunity and chose to remain in their homes, using wood stoves or generators for heat.  Many schools, a week and a half from the winter holiday break at the time of the storm, cancelled school for the remainder of the month because of a lack of electricity and clear routes for transporting students.  ||A public works employee, supervising storm clean up in Marlborough, went missing during his shift and was found dead two days later in a reservoir.  Because this occurred as a part of his job, following the storm, it was considered an indirect fatality.  Damage to the infrastructure in Massachusetts and New Hampshire amounted to roughly eighty million dollars.  This amount does not include damage to private property, which was unavailable at the time of this report.  The extent of the damage and number of people affected prompted the governors of both Massachusetts and New Hampshire to request federal assistance.  FEMA approved both requests.  President Bush issued a Major Disaster Declaration for Public Assistance for seven Massachusetts counties and all of New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half an inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces across eastern Hillsborough County.  Numerous wires down in Merrimack and Manchester.  Trees, wires, and utility poles downed in Pelham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved southeast of Nantucket immediately following another coastal storm that affected southern New England on the 19th and 20th of December. This storm spread snow across all of southern New England then switched over to rain across northern Connecticut, Rhode Island and portions of Massachusetts south of the Massachusetts Turnpike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to twelve inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the eastern Great Lakes combined with a developing low off the East Coast to produce a light to moderate snow across the state.  The greatest snow from the storm fell across northern Carroll County where 5 to 7 inches fell.  The remainder of the state generally received 2 to 5 inches of snow.

Well below normal temperatures caused a freeze-up jam to develop on the Sugar River at West Claremont, (flood stage 7.0 ft). The river briefly went above flood stage cresting at 7.2 ft, resulting in only minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused by a rapid freeze-up resulted in minor flooding on the Sugar River in West Claremont, (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 7.2 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through portions of northern and central New Hampshire during the late afternoon of June 10th. These storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and power lines in Hanover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through portions of northern and central New Hampshire during the late afternoon of June 10th. These storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and power lines in the town of Lyme.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of slow moving showers and thunderstorms produced up to 6 inches of rain in 3 hours resulting in flash flooding in several towns in southeast New Hampshire. Numerous roads were inundated by small streams and many were washed out. Several cars were trapped in rising water in Laconia and several homes were also damaged. Damage was also extensive in Meredith and New Hampton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Laconia area washing out several roads and damaging cars and homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved through the Great Lakes and into the St. Lawrence Valley on Tuesday, November 25th.  Snow fell north of the associated warm front as the low moved slowly northeast.  Warmer air eventually worked its way northward and changed the precipitation to rain.  Heavy snow fell across Coos and portions of Carroll Counties with 17 inches reported in Randolph.  Southern parts of the state had little or no snow as the precipitation fell mostly as rain.

An arctic cold front crossed the state bringing heavy snow to portions of the region.  Generally, 4 to 8 inches of snow fell in a period of a few hours as the front passed.

A powerful low pressure system tracked through eastern New York spreading heavy rain and strong winds across Southern New England. Heavy rain coinciding with spring snowmelt contributed to river flooding, especially along the Connecticut River. In addition, rainfall totals of two to three inches resulted in areal flooding across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads in Swanzey were flooded.  South Road was closed due to a mudslide.

A mesoscale convective system (MCS) moved across southern New Hampshire during the early morning hours resulting in reports of hail and damaging wind.
Afternoon heating combined with an approaching cold front created enough lift and instability ahead of it to produce showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southern New Hampshire became the focus for severe thunderstorm activity as a short wave approached the region from the west. Low level shear quickly increased during the late morning and early afternoon setting the stage for a major severe weather outbreak. Large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain were associated with these storms which began developing in the early afternoon and lasted into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Durham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a pine tree and travelled along the ground to a nearby shed.  The 8 x 10 ft. shed contained pool chemicals and gardening materials and exploded when struck.

Low pressure over the southeast United States and moisture from Hurricane Kyle resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rainfall in southeast New Hampshire. This caused mostly minor flooding with some road washouts in the Somersworth area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on small rivers and streams in the Somersworth area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna made landfall near the North Carolina/South Carolina border at 320AM EDT September 6.  Hanna then moved north/northeast across the Mid-Atlantic states and southern New England September 6 and 7.  Storm total rainfall in southern New England ranged from 2.04 inches in Randolph, MA to 6.56 inches in Nashua, NH.  The highest sustained wind of 27 knots (31 mph) was recorded at Nantucket Airport.  The highest wind gust of 37 knots (43 mph) was recorded at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, MA.  The lowest sea level pressure was 994.5 mb at Conimicut Lighthouse, RI.  A coastal storm surge of 2 feet above normal tide was experienced at Providence, RI.  No coastal flooding was reported.  Minor wind damage was sustained by nearly every county in southern New England.  Significant wind damage occurred in southern Bristol and southern Plymouth Counties in Massachusetts and southeastern Providence County in Rhode Island.  All of the associated effects of Hanna in Southern New England resulted in no loss of life or injuries and $939K in property damage.  The winds caused $23K in property damage.  Inland flooding resulted in $916K in property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with Tropical Storm Hanna ranged from 4.69 inches to 6.18 inches in western Hillsborough County.  This resulted in flooding.  In Goffstown, a thirty foot section of road washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southern New Hampshire became the focus for severe thunderstorm activity as a short wave approached the region from the west. Low level shear quickly increased during the late morning and early afternoon setting the stage for a major severe weather outbreak. Large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain were associated with these storms which began developing in the early afternoon and lasted into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna made landfall near the North Carolina/South Carolina border at 320AM EDT September 6.  Hanna then moved north/northeast across the Mid-Atlantic states and southern New England September 6 and 7.  Storm total rainfall in southern New England ranged from 2.04 inches in Randolph, MA to 6.56 inches in Nashua, NH.  The highest sustained wind of 27 knots (31 mph) was recorded at Nantucket Airport.  The highest wind gust of 37 knots (43 mph) was recorded at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, MA.  The lowest sea level pressure was 994.5 mb at Conimicut Lighthouse, RI.  A coastal storm surge of 2 feet above normal tide was experienced at Providence, RI.  No coastal flooding was reported.  Minor wind damage was sustained by nearly every county in southern New England.  Significant wind damage occurred in southern Bristol and southern Plymouth Counties in Massachusetts and southeastern Providence County in Rhode Island.  All of the associated effects of Hanna in Southern New England resulted in no loss of life or injuries and $939K in property damage.  The winds caused $23K in property damage.  Inland flooding resulted in $916K in property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall associated with Tropical Storm Hanna resulted in 4.79 inches to 6.56 inches of rain across Hillsborough County with Manchester receiving 4.79 inches.  Most of this rain fell between 6pm EDT and midnight which resulted in flash flooding across Manchester.  Numerous streets and basements were flooded with up to two feet of water: including Bridge, Beach, Central, and Chestnut Streets.  The police department garage was flooded with six feet of water and buckled an asphalt ramp that leads into the garage, making it unusable.  The floodwaters then ran into the firing range, gym, an office and an electrical system, causing the police station to have to use backup power from the fire department next door.  At least two police cars ended up with significant water damage.  All told the police station sustained at least $100,000 in damage.  The fire department used a rescue boat to rescue seven to ten people from submerged cars and one from a flooded home.  The Manchester Library's basement auditorium and a nearby room was flooded with up to six inches of water.  The worst flooding occurred in the Crosbie Street neighborhood where three houses were uninhabitable for several days and 13 others were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying coastal low spread heavy snow across southern New England. Snow began in the early afternoon across Connecticut and southwestern Massachusetts, spreading quickly across Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and southern New Hampshire. Eight to twelve inches of snow fell across much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying coastal low spread heavy snow across southern New England. Eight to twelve inches of snow fell across much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to eleven inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary dropped south of New England on the evening of the 10th. Low pressure developed along the frontal boundary across the southeastern states late on the night of the 10th into the 11th.  The low then tracked rapidly to the northeast, spreading a significant amount of precipitation into New England. A deep layer of warm air aloft and sub-freezing air at the surface resulted in a major ice storm across interior Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire as well as much of northern New England. ||The hardest hit areas in southern New England were the Monadnock region of southwest New Hampshire, the Worcester Hills in central Massachusetts, and the east slopes of the Berkshires in western Massachusetts. Anywhere from half an inch to an inch of ice accreted on many exposed surfaces. Especially when combined with breezy conditions, the ice downed numerous trees, branches, and power lines which resulted in widespread power outages. More than 300,000 customers were reportedly without power in Massachusetts and an additional 300,000 were without power in the state of New Hampshire. Because of the breadth of this storm (from Pennsylvania to Maine), extra crews to reinstate power were harder to come by.  Power crews from states as far away as South Carolina, as well as local National Guard teams, were called out to help with power restoration and clean up.  While most people had their power restored within a week, others were still without power at Christmas (nearly 2 weeks later). During this period, temperatures were mostly below normal and at least one major snowstorm affected the same area.  At the time of the December 19th snowstorm, over 100,000 customers were still without power in the two states combined.  Most communities had opened shelters to provide a warm, safe place for residents.  According to the numbers reported in local newspapers, most did not take advantage of this opportunity and chose to remain in their homes, using wood stoves or generators for heat.  Many schools, a week and a half from the winter holiday break at the time of the storm, cancelled school for the remainder of the month because of a lack of electricity and clear routes for transporting students.  ||A public works employee, supervising storm clean up in Marlborough, went missing during his shift and was found dead two days later in a reservoir.  Because this occurred as a part of his job, following the storm, it was considered an indirect fatality.  Damage to the infrastructure in Massachusetts and New Hampshire amounted to roughly eighty million dollars.  This amount does not include damage to private property, which was unavailable at the time of this report.  The extent of the damage and number of people affected prompted the governors of both Massachusetts and New Hampshire to request federal assistance.  FEMA approved both requests.  President Bush issued a Major Disaster Declaration for Public Assistance for seven Massachusetts counties and all of New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were downed in Rindge, Dublin, Jaffrey, Troy, and Swanzey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved southeast of Nantucket immediately following another coastal storm that affected southern New England on the 19th and 20th of December. This storm spread snow across all of southern New England then switched over to rain across northern Connecticut, Rhode Island and portions of Massachusetts south of the Massachusetts Turnpike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the mid Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 19th moved rapidly eastward during the day and well off the East Coast by the morning of the 20th.  The low brought heavy snow to most of the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches in northern Coos County to almost a foot in the higher terrain in southern Grafton County.  Most areas received 5 to 10 inches of snow from the storm.

An area of low pressure weakened as it crossed the Great Lakes on the morning of the 21st as a second area of low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast.  The coastal low intensified rapidly as it moved northeast along the East Coast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 22nd. The coastal low brought heavy snow to the entire state with amounts generally ranging from 10 to 15 inches with amounts ranging up to 24 inches in southern Coos County.

A low pressure system developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast and moved up the east coast southeast of Nantucket producing snow, rain, and ice across Southern New England. Widespread two to four inch rainfall amounts resulted in small stream and poor drainage flooding as well as some minor river flooding. In addition, there was some minor wind damage from strong northeast winds, especially along the coast.

The low was over Nova Scotia by 7 am, January 2nd. ||The storm brought heavy snow to much of the region, with a mix of snow and rain in Rockingham County.  Snowfall totals ranged from several inches in southern Rockingham County to 8 to 10 inches with locally higher amounts over Carroll, Grafton, and southern Coos Counties.  Northern Coos County generally received 3 to 6 inches of snow.

A major winter storm brought a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to New Hampshire from the morning of December 11th to the morning of December 12th.  The greatest impact in the state was in southern and central New Hampshire where a significant ice storm occurred.  Following the ice storm, recovery and restoration efforts were negatively impacted by additional winter weather events that passed through the state.  ||The ice storm was caused by an area of low pressure that moved northeastward along a stalled frontal boundary off the East Coast.  Near ground level, cold air from eastern Canada funneled southward into the region on northerly winds.  Southerly winds at higher levels of the atmosphere brought warmer (above freezing) air in aloft.  Across southern and central New Hampshire, the thermal structure of the atmosphere was conducive to produce freezing rain and sleet.   A mixture of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain began Thursday morning across the state. However, as the northerly winds strengthened at the surface and southerly winds strengthened aloft, the precipitation changed to mostly a mix of sleet and freezing rain in southern and central New Hampshire.  The freezing rain and sleet continued overnight and into Friday morning before ending.  While ���melted��� precipitation amounts across the southern and central part of the state ranged from 1 to 3 inches, ice accretion to trees and wires in these areas generally ranged from about a half inch to about an inch.  The weight of the ice caused branches to snap, and trees to either snap or uproot, and brought down power lines and poles across the region.  About 400 thousand utility customers lost power during the event, with some customers without power for two weeks.  Property damage across northern, central and southeastern New Hampshire was estimated at over $5 million.

High temperatures in the 50s over a period of several days resulted in enough runoff to cause ice on the Sugar River in West Claremont to move and jam. The River crested at 7.2 feet, 0.2 feet above the 7.0 foot flood stage.  Only minor flooding occurred during a 25 minute period.

Low pressure tracked across the midwest and redeveloped off the coast of Washington, D.C., tracking up the coast just east of Boston.  This spread six to eight inches of snow across southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and wires were downed in Keene near Gilsum Road.  Trees were downed onto wires in Richmond, Alstead, and Hinsdale.  In Hinsdale, downed wires burned on Meeting House Road and on Route 63.  Trees and wires were downed near a house on Russ Way in Swanzey and on Westmoreland Road in Chesterfield.  In addition, slick roads resulted in several tractor-trailers to jacknife and get stuck around Cheshire county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the midwest and redeveloped off the coast of Washington, D.C., tracking up the coast just east of Boston.  This spread six to eight inches of snow across southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in Bennington near Routes 31 and 202.  Wires were also downed on Route 137 in Hancock.

An area of slow moving showers and thunderstorms produced up to 6 inches of rain in 3 hours resulting in flash flooding on Ames Brook in Ashland which rose rapidly and flooded the Ames Brook Campground. A family trying to escape the flooded campground drove over a bridge on the brook just as it was washed away. Tragically a 7 year old girl was killed and her mother and brother were injured. In addition many roads were flooded or washed out in other areas of town. Dozens of residents had to be evacuated along Elm Street and surrounding streets in Ashland during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Ashland resulting in the death of a young girl and injuring her mother and brother as they tried to drive out of the flood waters in a local campground. Many roads were flooded or washed out in other areas of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving low pressure system moved through Southern New England bringing 4-10 inches of snow to the region. In addition, strong cold advection and pressure rises resulted in very cold temperatures, strong winds, and bitterly cold wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of four to eight inches of snow were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of six to eight inches of snow were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved up the Atlantic coast and spread snow across southern New England.  Heavy snow fell across much of southern New Hampshire, downing trees and power lines.  At Manchester-Boston Regional Airport, more than a dozen flights were cancelled.

Low pressure moved up the Atlantic coast and spread snow across southern New England.  Heavy snow fell across much of southern New Hampshire, downing trees and power lines.  At Manchester-Boston Regional Airport, more than a dozen flights were cancelled.

Several areas of low pressure moved along a quasi-stationary frontal boundary and produced a mixed precipitation event which changed to snow across the region as colder air filtered into the area.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in southern areas to about 10 inches across portions of northern Grafton and Carroll Counties and southern Coos County. Northern Coos County generally had 4 to 6 inches of snow.

The freezing rain and sleet continued overnight and into Friday morning before ending.  While ���melted��� precipitation amounts across the southern and central part of the state ranged from 1 to 3 inches, ice accretion to trees and wires in these areas generally ranged from about a half inch to about an inch.  The weight of the ice caused branches to snap, and trees to either snap or uproot, and brought down power lines and poles across the region.  About 400 thousand utility customers lost power during the event, with some customers without power for two weeks.  Property damage across northern, central and southeastern New Hampshire was estimated at over $5 million.

A low pressure system developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast and moved up the east coast southeast of Nantucket producing snow, rain, and ice across Southern New England. Widespread two to four inch rainfall amounts resulted in small stream and poor drainage flooding as well as some minor river flooding. In addition, there was some minor wind damage from strong northeast winds, especially along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half an inch of ice accumulated in Jaffrey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved south of Long Island bring heavy snow to southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Bible Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 26th moved east to the mid-Atlantic States, then northeast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 27th.  The storm brought 6 to 12 inches of snow to most of the region with a mix of rain and snow in the southeastern part of the state.

Strong low pressure moved up the Appalachain Mountains spreading heavy rain over the area. Rainfall totals of 2 inches and mild temperatures caused ice to move and jam on the Saco River in Conway.  Moving ice also caused an ice jam and some flooding on the Ammonoosuc River in Bath. Flooding in both locations was localized and minor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Saco River in Conway resulted in minor localized flooding for a short time on the 9th of March.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused minor flooding on the Ammonoosuc River in Bath. Abbott Avenue from Mt Gardiner Road, to 3/4 of a mile downstream, was flooded by several feet of water. Large chunks of ice caused damage to the road. The road remained closed for 3 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist northwest upslope flow on the back side of an area of low pressure brought heavy snow to portions of the mountains. Amounts were quite variable across the region with 6 to 14 inches of snow reported in Coos County.

Low pressure moved northeast from the Ohio River Valley and into New England, spreading heavy rain over the region. Rainfall began on the afternoon of the 28th and ended 24 hours later. Rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches. The heavy rain was enough to cause minor flooding on the Saco and Pemigewasset Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in minor flooding on the Saco River, (flood stage 9.0 ft.), which crested at 9.96 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved eastward from the Great Lakes into Northern New England, eroding a mid level cap associated with a subtropical ridge over Southern New England. This allowed widespread showers and thunderstorms to develop over the area resulting in numerous reports of wind damage throughout southern New England and the adjacent marine zones. ||Despite the amount of damage the storms caused, they did bring some relief from a few days of record high temperatures across New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.  In addition, lightning struck a house on Mason Road.  No damage was reported with the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in western New Hampshire during the late morning hours of June 22nd and spread eastward during the early afternoon. These storms produced damaging winds and hail across central and southern New Hampshire. Baseball size hail was reported at Mt Cardigan in Orange.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Center Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southern New England was under the favorable left front quadrant of a strong upper level jet as a shortwave moved across the region.  This shortwave provided enough lift in an already unstable atmosphere to produce showers and thunderstorms across much of the area.  Most of the events that occurred were hail though there were a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds, including one tree that was fell onto a car.

The officer was knocked down and momentarily incapacitated by the strike, and then taken to a nearby hospital, where he was checked out and then released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive, long lived severe thunderstorm developed in the early afternoon hours of June 17th in central Carroll County and shifted eastward into western Maine by mid afternoon. Large hail was the main product of this storm with much of it falling in the Conway area. Other isolated severe cells later in the day dropped more hail near Conway and farther south in Strafford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail in Conway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through portions of northern and central New Hampshire during the late afternoon of June 10th. These storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced nickel size hail in North Sandwich.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced nickel size hail just north of Gorham. A funnel cloud was also reported at the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in northern New Hampshire on the evening of June 16th. Pea to a little larger than dime-size hail covered the ground up to 1 inch deep in Groveton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced up to .75 inch hail which covered the ground to a depth of 1 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire that damaged a shed in Rochester.  The lightning apparently struck a nearby tree and followed the roots to the shed.  The fire damaged the side of the shed and some of the contents.  Damage was estimated at $500.
Lightning struck a two-story apartment building in Colebrook and the resultant fire heavily damaged the structure.  Two families were displaced by the fire.  Damage was estimated at $200,000.
A lee side trough was a focus for showers and thunderstorms, many becoming severe and producing damaging winds and heavy rain.
A lee side trough was a focus for showers and thunderstorms, many becoming severe and producing damaging winds and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires on Sunset Circle in Alstead were downed by thunderstorm winds.  In addition, trees on South Street in Walpole were downed by thunderstorm winds onto a car.  Trees and wires on Route 12 in Walpole were downed by thunderstorm winds, blocking the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the fire department's radio system.  Backup radio systems were online within 5 minutes of the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moved through southern New Hampshire.  Numerous lightning strikes were observed with these storms, including one that caused damage to a home in Marlborough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house in Marlborough was struck by lightning.  It resulted in a three foot diameter hole through the wall of the house, melting some wires.

On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and central New Hampshire. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. Sunshine began breaking out to the east of this boundary by mid morning and convection began developing by late morning. Storms grew rapidly and quickly began rotating. A tornado rated as strong as F2 cut a 50 mile path through 5 counties in southeast New Hampshire resulting in 1 fatality and damage to over 100 structures some of which were completely destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees near Ossipee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable airmass remained over New Hampshire on the 19th of July with a stalled frontal boundary just north of the state in southern Quebec. Thunderstorms developed on an instability axis across south central portions of the state around mid afternoon. Large hail and damaging winds were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail near Winnisquam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through Southern New England, sparking showers and thunderstorms.  Most of the thunderstorms produced only minor damage from winds and a smattering of large hail, one of the thunderstorms wreaked havoc on the small town of Hinsdale, NH.  There, trees and utility poles were downed all over town, landing on trailers, cars and houses.  Trees were also downed on the towns busiest commuter road, Route 119, disrupting traffic during rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen utility poles in North Hinsdale were downed by thunderstorm winds.  Other wind related damage in Hinsdale, includes, trees and wires downed in Indian Acres, a tree downed on a trailer and a car, and several ten inch diameter branches were snapped off by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through southern New England and produced a long line of showers and thunderstorms that tracked east across the area.  Much of this line of storms was severe with damaging thunderstorm winds and occasional hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Hartford Drive, Berkley Street, Castleton Court, and Regal Drive were downed by thunderstorm winds.  At least one tree was downed across a driveway.  Another was downed onto a house and another was downed onto a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through southern New England and produced a long line of showers and thunderstorms that tracked east across the area.  Much of this line of storms was severe with damaging thunderstorm winds and occasional hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house, starting a fire in the attic.  The house suffered minimal fire, smoke, and water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and a warm, humid, unstable atmosphere combined to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England.  These storms produced damaging wind and lots of lightning.  Seven to ten people were struck when standing under a tree at a soccer game in Dorchester, MA.  Another was struck when turning off the water to an outdoor shower in Hingham, MA.  Several other lightning strikes ignited fires in New Hampshire and Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Route 123 and Route 9 in South Stoddard were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and a warm, humid, unstable atmosphere combined to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England.  These storms produced damaging wind and lots of lightning.  Seven to ten people were struck when standing under a tree at a soccer game in Dorchester, MA.  Another was struck when turning off the water to an outdoor shower in Hingham, MA.  Several other lightning strikes ignited fires in New Hampshire and Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house on Green Mountain Road was struck by lightning and caught fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers and thunderstorms developed on a sea breeze boundary.  There was enough moisture in the atmosphere that heavy rain accompanied one of these thunderstorms in Nashua.  One and a half inches of rain fell in half an hour at Nashua Memorial Airport.  This resulted in flash flooding across portions of Nashua.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observers reported one and a half inches of rain fell at the Nashua Municipal Airport in half an hour.  This resulted in flash flooding across much of the city.  In the area of Bowers Street, water depth was between one and one and a half feet.  This flooded the lower level of the YMCA and forced firefighters to close a portion of the road.  One person was trapped in a car after driving into water that had pooled on Daniel Webster Highway.  The car was towed out by a good Samaritan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through Southern New England, sparking showers and thunderstorms.  Most of the thunderstorms produced only minor damage from winds and a smattering of large hail, one of the thunderstorms wreaked havoc on the small town of Hinsdale, NH.  There, trees and utility poles were downed all over town, landing on trailers, cars and houses.  Trees were also downed on the towns busiest commuter road, Route 119, disrupting traffic during rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.  One landed on a car, another landed on a house on Spring Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable airmass remained over New Hampshire on the 19th of July with a stalled frontal boundary just north of the state in southern Quebec. Thunderstorms developed on an instability axis across south central portions of the state around mid afternoon. Large hail and damaging winds were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail in Sanbornton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through southern New England and produced a long line of showers and thunderstorms that tracked east across the area.  Much of this line of storms was severe with damaging thunderstorm winds and occasional hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house, igniting a gas line, and starting a fire.  The lightning strike also hit a water line coming into the house, which flooded the basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in the town of Epsom during the afternoon of July 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Epsom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in Rockingham County during the evening of July 3rd. Trees were reported down in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in South Hampton. Trees were also reported down in Kingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers and thunderstorms developed on a sea breeze boundary.  There was enough moisture in the atmosphere that heavy rain accompanied one of these thunderstorms in Nashua.  One and a half inches of rain fell in half an hour at Nashua Memorial Airport.  This resulted in flash flooding across portions of Nashua.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on New Searles Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures aloft preceded a cold front through southern New England.  This increased instability in the area and contributed to large hail forming in thunderstorms that occurred across the area.  These storms largely affected southeastern Massachusetts and the coastal waters east of Cape Cod.  The only wind damage that occurred was in the Myles Standish State Forest in Plymouth where numerous trees were downed by strong winds.

Cold temperatures aloft preceded a cold front through southern New England.  This increased instability in the area and contributed to large hail forming in thunderstorms that occurred across the area.  These storms largely affected southeastern Massachusetts and the coastal waters east of Cape Cod.  The only wind damage that occurred was in the Myles Standish State Forest in Plymouth where numerous trees were downed by strong winds.

An unstable atmosphere and plenty of sunshine led to widespread severe weather across Southern New England.  Cold upper level temperatures allowed large hail to form and form it did with one report of two inch diameter hail in Marlborough, Massachusetts.  There was heavy rain associated with some of the storms as well which resulted in some urban flooding and some flash flooding in Worcester, Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of tropical storm Hanna moved through eastern New England dumping 3 to 6 inches of rain in New Hampshire in about 8 hours causing rapid rises on area streams. Road washouts were the major problem. Several people were evacuated from Epsom Valley and Lazy River Campground in Epsom.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from the remnants of tropical storm Hanna resulted in flooding on small rivers and streams in the Epsom area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Newland Street was downed by thunderstorm winds onto a car.

A cold front moving across southern New England combined with a low level jet to produce showers and thunderstorms.  Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a structure on Old Town Farm Road resulting in a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across southern New England combined with a low level jet to produce showers and thunderstorms.  Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree, then a gas line, causing a small fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms produced 3 to 4 inches (radar estimated) of rain in a 3 hour period causing flash flooding of the Gale River and Black Brook in Franconia. Roads were washed out in Thorton and Waterville Valley as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flashed flooding which washed out roads in Thornton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low tracked across southern Ontario producing showers and thunderstorms across southern New England.  These storms produced a waterspout and very heavy rains resulting in flash flooding in southeastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house on Laurelcrest Drive was struck by lightning and caught fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced several reports of large hail in southeast New Hampshire during the early evening of August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .88 hail in East Derry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced several reports of large hail in southeast New Hampshire during the early evening of August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Derry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and central New Hampshire. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. Sunshine began breaking out to the east of this boundary by mid morning and convection began developing by late morning. Storms grew rapidly and quickly began rotating. A tornado rated as strong as F2 cut a 50 mile path through 5 counties in southeast New Hampshire resulting in 1 fatality and damage to over 100 structures some of which were completely destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An E-F2 tornado moving north northeast out of Rockingham County crossed into Merrimack County near Route 202 and continued north northeast a little over 5 miles before entering Belknap County. Homes and buildins in the tornadoes path sustained damage along the path which was up to a half mile wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and central New Hampshire. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. Sunshine began breaking out to the east of this boundary by mid morning and convection began developing by late morning. Storms grew rapidly and quickly began rotating. A tornado rated as strong as F2 cut a 50 mile path through 5 counties in southeast New Hampshire resulting in 1 fatality and damage to over 100 structures some of which were completely destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An E-F2 tornado moving north northeast out of Rockingham County entered Belknap County about 2 miles southwest of South Barnstead near Province Road. The storm travaled almost 12 miles before crossing into Strafford County resulting in E-F0 to E-F2 damage. There were numerous houses and buildings that were damaged or destroyed by the tornado or by falling trees. In addition, there were thousands of downed trees and numerous power lines down along the path of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and central New Hampshire. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An E-F2 tornado moving out of Strafford County crossed into Carroll 2.9 miles east of South Wolfboro and continued to skip north northeast for almost 20 miles through the town of Freedom. F0 to F2 damage occurred along the path of the storm and many thousands of trees were blown down. Cars, homes and other structures were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and central New Hampshire. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An E-F1 tornado, moving north northeast out of Belknap County, entered Strafford County approximately 2.2 miles north northwest of New Durham. The storm skipped along for more than 8 miles before exiting into Carroll County. The intensity of the tornado varied between F0 and F2, and numerous trees were blown down along the path of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and central New Hampshire. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An E-F2 tornado touched down about 5 miles southwest of Northwood Narrows and moved north northeast for a little over 5 miles before crossing into Merrimack County. Numerous trees were downed and many homes were damaged or destroyed.  A woman was killed when the house she was in collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a fire that burned down a house in Newington.
A slow moving area of low pressure produced 3 inches of rainfall in Grafton County New Hampshire. Minor flooding resulted on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock during the evening of July 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure produced 3 inches of rain resulting in minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River, (flood stage 9.0 ft), which crested at 9.19 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed over southeast New Hampshire during the evening of July 31st. Large hail was associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Allenstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed over southeast New Hampshire during the evening of July 31st. Large hail was associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Deerfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches in 12 hours caused minor flooding on the Pemigewassett River at Woodstock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused minor flooding on the Pemigewassett River at Woodstock flood stage 9.0 ft) which crested after a few hours at 9.53 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southern New England, bringing rain, a few thunderstorms, and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in Jaffrey, Rindge, Swanzey, Winchester, Dublin, Alstead, Walpole, Chesterfield, and Westmoreland.  Two of the trees fell on houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure to the west of the forecast area and a weak southerly flow aloft set the stage for a major outbreak of severe thunderstorms which produced numerous reports of large hail in central and southern New Hampshire. Some of the larger storms also brought down trees and power lines. In addition, these slow moving storms produced very heavy rainfall and resulted in several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Laconia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper level low pressure system swinging into the northeast on on the 16th of August resulted in low freezing levels and weak upper flow. A strong vorticity maximum advecting into the area combined with marginal cape indices to fire off the initial convection. Terrain and potent outflow boundaries organized these initial cells into longer lived pulse type storms which quickly became numerous throughout the forecast area. Large hail was the main result of these storms with numerous reports of hail 1 inch or larger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced multiple reports .75 inch hail in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dumped 2 to 3 inches of rain in east central New Hampshire and caused many road washouts on Route 113 in Holderness, which in turn brought down power lines. River Road in Dorchester was also washed out and impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding which washed out portions of Route 113 in Holderness.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure to the west of the forecast area and a weak southerly flow aloft set the stage for a major outbreak of severe thunderstorms which produced numerous reports of large hail in central and southern New Hampshire. Some of the larger storms also brought down trees and power lines. In addition, these slow moving storms produced very heavy rainfall and resulted in several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees 7 miles northeast of Monroe blocking Route 135 near Dam Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in western New Hampshire during the late morning hours of June 22nd and spread eastward during the early afternoon. These storms produced damaging winds and hail across central and southern New Hampshire. Baseball size hail was reported at Mt Cardigan in Orange.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on wires in East Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across portions of southern New Hampshire on June 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorms downed trees in Henniker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable airmass remained over New Hampshire on the 19th of July with a stalled frontal boundary just north of the state in southern Quebec. Thunderstorms developed on an instability axis across south central portions of the state around mid afternoon. Large hail and damaging winds were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on a house in Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper level low pressure system swinging into the northeast on on the 16th of August resulted in low freezing levels and weak upper flow. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southern New Hampshire became the focus for severe thunderstorm activity as a short wave approached the region from the west. Low level shear quickly increased during the late morning and early afternoon setting the stage for a major severe weather outbreak. Large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain were associated with these storms which began developing in the early afternoon and lasted into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees in Wolfeboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England resulting in showers and thunderstorms.  Cold upper level temperatures created an unstable atmosphere and allowed large hail to form in these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel sized hail fell in Nashua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front provided a focus for severe thunderstorms.  These thunderstorms produced very heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding, hail, and damaging winds.
